title: Ultrafast decoupling of quasiparticles and spin fluctuations in superconducting cuprates
authors: Yuto Taniguchi, Ryo Kato, Tatsuya Amano, Hirotake Itoh, Yohei Kawakam, Yuto Nakamura, Hideo Kishida, Christian Bernhard, Jure Demsar, Takahiko Sasaki, Terukazu Nishizaki, Kenji Yonemitsu, Shinichiro Iwai
abstract: Understanding how quasiparticles are generated following a rapid quench of superconductivity in high-Tc cuprates is a key unresolved problem in nonequilibrium superconductivity. Here we resolve these processes in optimally doped YBCO [YBa2Cu3Oy(y=6.94, Tc=92 K)] using broadband (0.16 -4.1 eV, ca. 100 fs) and nearly single-cycle (6 fs) transient reflectivity spectroscopy. We show that within a few femtosecond, enhanced electron-electron Umklapp scattering dominates, signaling a transient modulation of long-range Coulomb interactions on the eV scale. This regime is followed by a rapid suppression of the scattering rate of the mid-infrared absorption associated with carriers dressed by spin fluctuations. We attribute this observation to an ultrafast decoupling of quasiparticles from the spin-fluctuation background, occurring on a 90 fs timescale set by the inverse optical gap. These findings reveal the correlated many-body dynamics underlying quasiparticle generation in cuprates and provide further clues for unconventional pairing mechanism.
summary: This paper uses ultrafast spectroscopy to resolve the initial stages of quasiparticle generation in YBCO. It reveals that electron-electron Umklapp scattering occurs almost instantaneously, followed by a 90 fs decoupling of quasiparticles from spin fluctuations. These findings offer new clues regarding the pairing mechanism in high-temperature superconductors.
main_problem: Understanding the ultrafast dynamics of quasiparticle generation and the decoupling of quasiparticles from spin fluctuations following a rapid quench of superconductivity in high-Tc cuprates.
main_result: The study identifies an initial enhancement of electron-electron Umklapp scattering within a few femtoseconds, followed by an ultrafast decoupling of quasiparticles from the spin-fluctuation background on a 90 fs timescale.
method: Ultrafast broadband (0.16-4.1 eV) and near single-cycle (6 fs) transient reflectivity spectroscopy using pump-probe techniques.
model_or_system: Optimally doped YBCO (YBa2Cu3Oy, y=6-6.94) single crystals, analyzed using a Drude-Lorentz model and the three-band Hubbard (Emery) model context.
key_observables: Transient reflectivity (delta R/R), oscillator strengths (f), scattering rates (gamma), and central frequencies (omega) of various electronic bands.
important_parameters: Temporal scales of <10 fs, 90 fs, and 2 ps; energy scales including the mid-IR band (0.1-1 eV), 1.4 eV band, and ZRS band (~1.9 eV).
main_assumptions: The low-frequency response in the superconducting state is approximated with a Drude term, and the Drude-Lorentz model parameters are assumed to be uniform within the layers of a multilayer film model.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the steady-state permittivity spectrum; Fig 2 shows the time evolution of delta R/R at 10 K; Fig 3 presents high-resolution temporal dynamics using 6 fs pulses; Fig 4/S-series show Drude-Lorentz parameter evolution and spectral analysis.
paper_structure: Introduction to the problem of quasiparticle generation, experimental setup description, steady-state spectral identification, results of broadband and ultrafast spectroscopy in both SC and normal states, and discussion of the physical mechanisms (Umklapp scattering and Hartree shift).
why_interesting: The paper provides high-resolution insights into many-body dynamics, specifically the non-equilibrium decoupling of particles from a bosonic (spin-fluctuation) background, which is relevant to the study of driven many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig.1 Steady-state permittivity spectrum in optimally doped YBa2Cu3O6.94 (20 K). Steady-state permittivity spectrum in optimally doped YBa2Cu3O6.94 (20 K). The origins of the mid-IR band (orange shade), 1.4 eV band (red shade x5), and ZRS band (green shade x7) are also schematically shown, i.e., carriers accompanied by spin fluctuations (mid-IR band), plasma responses associated with long-range Coulomb interactions (1.4 eV band) and Umklapp scatterings, and Zhang–Rice singlets (ZRS band).

Fig.3 (a) Spectrally integrated time evolution of ΔR/R (The red curve shows the cross-correlation of pump and probe pulses) and (b) time evolution of ΔR/R spectrum, measured using a 6 fs pulse. Polarizations of pump (Epu) and probe (Epr) pulses are parallel to the a-axis (6 K, Iex= 0.1 mJ/cm²). (c)-(f) ΔR/R spectra at td=-10, 0 fs (c), 10 fs (d), 90 fs (e), 200 fs(f) are shown. The crosses and solid lines show the normalized ΔR/R spectra measured by 100 fs pulses and the results of the DLM analysis (shown in Figs. 2(c)(d) and 4(a)(b)), respectively.

Fig.2 Time evolution of transient reflectivity(ΔR/R) at 10 K. (a) Two- dimensional plot of the time evolution of ΔR/R spectrum, measured using a 100 fs pulse (10 K, pump energy of 0.89 eV, Iex= 0.04 mJ/cm²). Polarizations of pump (Epu) and probe (Epr) pulses are parallel to the a-axis. (b) Steady-state R spectrum and (c)(d) ΔR/R spectra measured at td=0 ps (c), 0.2 ps (d). The spectral range below 1 eV is shown multiplied by 3. (e) Time evolutions of ΔR/R measured at (I) 0.62 eV (mid-IR band), (II) 1.17 eV (peak of ΔR/R), (III) 1.90 eV (ZRS band), and (IV) 2.92 eV (CT band).

Fig.4 Fig. 4 Results of Drude-Lorentz (DLM) analysis (10 K). (a)(b) Results of the DLM analysis for the ΔR/R spectra at td = 0 ps (a) and 0.2 ps (b) are presented as the solid lines. (c)-(i) Time evolutions of γMIR and fMIR [(c) and (f)], γ1.4 eV and f1.4 eV [(d) and (g)], and γZRS and fZRS [(e) and (i)]. (h) Time evolution of the peak shift of the 1.4 eV band (1.4 eV).

Fig.5 (a)(b) Schematic illustrations of Hartree shifts of the CT energy in the SC state (a) and the normal state (b). The size of the circles indicate the hole densities of 3d and 2p orbitals. (c) Relation between the CT energy[𝑝𝑑= 𝑝−𝑑 with the on-site energy of the O 2p (Cu 3d) orbital 𝑝 (𝑑) in the hole picture] and fZRS calculated based on the exact diagonalization [25]. The parameters and boundary conditions used here are the same as those in the 1-hole case of Fig. 12(b) in Reference 25 except for the CT energy.
